summary: Water (hydrogen) dissolved in nominally anhydrous minerals (NAMs) such as olivine and wadsleyite significantly enhances seismic attenuation and reduces seismic velocities at upper-mantle conditions. Even tens of ppm H/Si water content can increase Q^(-1) by an order of magnitude relative to dry aggregates, because dissolved hydrogen accelerates diffusion-assisted grain-boundary sliding and dislocation relaxation processes. This makes water content a critical parameter for interpreting low-velocity zones and high-attenuation regions in the upper mantle and transition zone.

key_papers.title: Effect of water on seismic attenuation of wadsleyite
key_papers.journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth

physical_mechanism: Dissolved water (as hydroxyl point defects, primarily (4H)_Si^x and (2H)_Mg^x) enhances seismic attenuation in NAMs through several interconnected mechanisms: (1) Proton diffusion enhancement of grain-boundary sliding: Water-derived hydrogen increases the mobility of point defects at and near grain boundaries, effectively lowering the viscosity of the grain-boundary region. This accelerates elastically-accommodated grain-boundary sliding (EAGBS), shifting the absorption band to higher frequencies (shorter relaxation times) and increasing Q^(-1) at seismic frequencies. (2) Dislocation-related relaxation: Protons pin to and interact with dislocation cores and jogs, modifying the dislocation damping spectrum. Water-weakened dislocation glide and climb facilitates anelastic strain recovery, contributing an additional dissipation mechanism particularly at higher temperatures. (3) Diffusion-assisted creep coupling: At high homologous temperatures, the enhanced silicon and oxygen diffusion rates in hydrous olivine promote diffusion creep and associated transient (anelastic) deformation, broadening the high-temperature background attenuation. (4) The net effect is a reduction of the effective activation energy for anelastic relaxation by approximately 20-60 kJ/mol compared to dry conditions, meaning that a given level of attenuation is reached at lower temperatures in hydrous minerals. The proton diffusion mechanism (H+ hopping between oxygen sites with activation energy ~120-150 kJ/mol) is orders of magnitude faster than silicon or oxygen diffusion, making it the rate-controlling step for water-enhanced grain-boundary processes at moderate temperatures (800-1200 degrees C).
mathematical_formulation: The influence of water on attenuation is commonly parameterized by modifying the relaxation timescale in the absorption band model: (1) Water-dependent relaxation time: tau(T,P,C_OH,d) = tau_0 * (d/d_ref)^m * (C_OH/C_ref)^(-r) * exp((E* + P*V*)/(R*T)), where C_OH is the water content (ppm H/Si or wt ppm H2O), r is the water-content exponent (typically r ~ 0.5-2.0), and other symbols have their usual meanings. (2) The standard extended Burgers or Andrade Q model is then applied with this modified timescale: Q^(-1)(omega,T,P,d,C_OH) = integral over relaxation times of D(tau) * omega*tau/(1 + (omega*tau)^2) dtau, where the distribution D(tau) shifts to shorter timescales with increasing C_OH. (3) Simplified power-law form (Karato, 2006): Q^(-1) = A * d^(-m) * C_OH^r * omega^(-alpha) * exp(-(E* + P*V*)/(R*T)), where A is a pre-exponential constant, alpha ~ 0.2-0.3 is the frequency exponent, and the water exponent r is determined experimentally. (4) Velocity correction follows from the Kramers-Kronig relation: V(omega)/V_0 = 1 - (1/2) * cot(alpha*pi/2) * Q^(-1)(omega). (5) For the specific case of olivine, the activation energy E* decreases from ~400-500 kJ/mol (dry) to ~300-400 kJ/mol (wet, ~100-1000 ppm H/Si), or equivalently the pre-exponential factor increases by a factor proportional to C_OH^r.
experimental_conditions: Key experimental measurements on the effect of water on seismic-frequency attenuation include: Temperature: 800-1300 degrees C; Pressure: 0.1-0.3 GPa in gas-medium apparatus (Paterson rig at ANU), up to 10-20 GPa for transition-zone mineral studies in multi-anvil press; Frequency: 0.001-1 Hz (forced torsional oscillation for shear attenuation); Grain size: 2-50 micrometers (hot-pressed synthetic olivine or natural dunite aggregates); Water content: 0 (dry, vacuum-fired) to ~1000 ppm H/Si (up to ~0.1 wt% H2O for olivine; up to 2-3 wt% H2O for wadsleyite); Composition: Fo90 olivine polycrystals (sol-gel or crushed San Carlos olivine), natural Anita Bay dunite, and synthetic wadsleyite; Measurement technique: forced torsional oscillation at sub-resonance frequencies with simultaneous measurement of shear modulus and attenuation (tan delta), FTIR spectroscopy for pre- and post-experiment water content determination, and EBSD for microstructural characterization. Controlling water content is challenging because hydrogen can diffuse out during high-temperature experiments; encapsulation and fugacity buffering (Ni-NiO, Fe-FeO) are used to stabilize water content and redox state.

key_parameters.name: Water content (C_OH)
key_parameters.description: Dissolved hydrogen concentration in the mineral; primary control on water-enhanced attenuation

key_parameters.name: Water content exponent (r)
key_parameters.description: Exponent relating Q^(-1) to C_OH; experimentally poorly constrained due to difficulty in controlling water fugacity

key_parameters.name: Activation energy - dry (E*_dry)
key_parameters.description: Activation energy for anelastic relaxation in dry olivine

key_parameters.name: Activation energy - wet (E*_wet)
key_parameters.description: Reduced activation energy for anelastic relaxation in hydrous olivine

key_parameters.name: Proton diffusion activation energy (E*_H)
key_parameters.description: Activation energy for hydrogen diffusion in olivine along the fastest direction [100]

key_parameters.name: Frequency exponent (alpha)
key_parameters.description: Exponent in the power-law frequency dependence of Q^(-1); may be weakly affected by water content

key_parameters.name: Grain-size exponent (m)
key_parameters.description: Exponent for grain-size dependence of relaxation time; similar for wet and dry conditions

key_parameters.name: Activation volume (V*)
key_parameters.description: Pressure dependence of anelastic relaxation; poorly constrained for hydrous conditions

key_parameters.name: Water storage capacity of olivine
key_parameters.description: Maximum water solubility in olivine at upper-mantle conditions; limits the water-enhanced attenuation effect

velocity_correction_magnitude: For Vs: Water-enhanced attenuation in olivine can increase the anelastic Vs correction from ~1-2% (dry, 1300 degrees C, 1 Hz) to ~3-6% (wet with ~200-500 ppm H/Si) at the same conditions, representing a factor of 2-3 increase. At lower frequencies (0.01 Hz, surface waves), the correction can reach 5-10% for hydrous olivine at asthenospheric temperatures. Karato & Jung (1998) estimated that water contents of 100-1000 ppm H/Si in the asthenosphere could account for Vs reductions of 3-8% without requiring partial melt. For Vp: The Vp correction is smaller, typically 40-50% of the Vs correction, giving ~1-3% Vp reduction for hydrous olivine at asthenospheric conditions. For wadsleyite in the transition zone: with water contents of 0.1-1 wt% H2O, Vs reductions of 2-5% are expected, potentially explaining the high-attenuation anomalies observed in the transition zone beneath subduction zones. The Vp/Vs ratio is expected to increase slightly with water content due to the preferential reduction of shear modulus. [uncertain]
implications_for_composition: Water content profoundly affects the interpretation of seismic velocities in the mantle: (1) LVZ origin: The competing hypotheses of partial melt vs. sub-solidus hydrous attenuation for the seismic low-velocity zone at 80-200 km depth depend critically on the water content of olivine. If the asthenosphere contains 200-800 ppm H/Si of water, sub-solidus anelastic effects alone may explain the observed velocity reduction without requiring partial melt, fundamentally changing the inferred thermal structure and volatile budget. (2) Temperature estimation bias: Neglecting water-enhanced attenuation leads to overestimation of mantle temperatures from seismic velocities by approximately 50-200 degrees C, because the velocity reduction attributed to temperature alone is partly caused by water-enhanced dissipation. (3) Transition zone water content: Wadsleyite and ringwoodite can hold 1-3 wt% H2O. If the transition zone is hydrated, the enhanced attenuation would reduce seismic velocities, potentially mimicking a thermal anomaly or compositional variation. This is relevant for interpreting the 410 km and 660 km discontinuity structures. (4) Lateral heterogeneity: Variations in water content (e.g., dehydration above subducting slabs vs. dry cratonic lithosphere) produce lateral variations in attenuation and velocity that can be misinterpreted as temperature or compositional heterogeneity if water effects are not modeled. (5) Trade-off with iron content: Both water and iron increase attenuation and reduce velocity; separating their effects requires joint constraints from attenuation (Q) and velocity, or independent constraints from electrical conductivity.
limitations: Major limitations and uncertainties include: (1) Poorly constrained water exponent: The exponent r relating Q^(-1) to C_OH is experimentally uncertain (ranging from 0.5 to 2.0 in different studies), because controlling and measuring water fugacity at high P-T conditions is extremely difficult. Water loss during experiments is a persistent problem. (2) Redox-water coupling: Cline et al. (2018) demonstrated that oxidation state independently affects attenuation in olivine, and previous studies that attributed attenuation enhancement to water may have partly reflected redox effects. Separating water from redox contributions remains an open challenge. (3) Limited pressure range: Most forced-oscillation experiments are conducted at 0.1-0.3 GPa, far below actual upper-mantle pressures (3-14 GPa). The pressure dependence of water-enhanced attenuation (activation volume for the hydrous mechanism) is essentially unknown. (4) Water speciation: Hydrogen can be incorporated as different point-defect species ((4H)_Si, (2H)_Mg, H associated with Fe^3+, Ti-clinohumite defects), each potentially contributing differently to attenuation. Most experimental studies do not resolve the contribution of individual defect types. (5) Grain-boundary vs. lattice water: The partition of water between grain interiors and grain boundaries, and their respective contributions to attenuation, is not well characterized. (6) Extrapolation to transition-zone minerals: Very few attenuation measurements exist for hydrous wadsleyite or ringwoodite, and the mechanisms may differ from olivine. (7) Natural water content uncertainty: The actual water content of the asthenosphere and transition zone is debated (estimates range from 50 to 1000+ ppm H/Si for the asthenosphere), introducing large uncertainty in forward-modeling seismic properties. (8) Frequency extrapolation: Laboratory frequencies (mHz-Hz) must be extrapolated to teleseismic and normal-mode frequencies, and the shape of the absorption band may change with water content.
implementation_notes: For implementation in ThermoElasticCalculator: (1) Inputs required: water content (C_OH in ppm H/Si or wt ppm H2O), mineral phase (olivine, wadsleyite, ringwoodite, etc.), temperature, pressure, grain size, and seismic frequency. The user should also specify whether to use dry or hydrous attenuation parameters. (2) Recommended approach: Modify the existing anelastic correction module (extended Burgers or Andrade model) by adding a water-dependent scaling factor to the relaxation time: tau_wet = tau_dry * (C_OH/C_ref)^(-r). Use r = 1.0 as default with an option for the user to vary it within 0.5-2.0 to assess sensitivity. Alternatively, reduce the effective activation energy by Delta_E = r * R * T * ln(C_OH/C_ref) for a simplified implementation. (3) The water storage capacity of each mineral phase should be checked against the input C_OH to ensure physical consistency (warn if C_OH exceeds solubility at given P,T). (4) Outputs: Q_S, Q_P, corrected Vs, corrected Vp at specified frequency, plus the incremental velocity correction attributable to water (difference between wet and dry corrections). (5) Acceptable approximations: For a first implementation, assume r = 1.0 and use a single reduced activation energy for wet conditions. A more sophisticated version could implement the full Karato (2006) formulation with explicit water-fugacity dependence. (6) The implementation should support toggling water effects on/off independently of melt effects, and allow combined water + melt scenarios. (7) For transition-zone minerals, use separate parameter sets (different E*, alpha, and C_OH ranges) but the same functional form.
